output:
# ISOMIX and CALIBER: Curing the Isotropic-Mixing-Rate Limit of FedMix with a Heterogeneity-Conditional, Exactly-Collapsing Federated Data-Augmentation Map

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Federated learning (FL) lets many clients train a shared model while their raw data never leaves the device: FedAvg [1] established the now-standard recipe of local SGD plus periodic parameter averaging, and the privacy contract -- data stays local, only model statistics are exchanged -- is the defining constraint of the field [15]. The central obstacle to that recipe is statistical heterogeneity. When client data is Non-IID, local updates drift away from one another and the averaged model degrades [2,9,22]; the heterogeneous-FL survey [4] frames this label/feature/quantity skew as the core open challenge, and empirical studies confirm that Dirichlet-controlled label skew sharply lowers federated accuracy [10].

A large body of work attacks heterogeneity at the optimizer or aggregation layer -- proximal regularization (FedProx [5]), control variates (SCAFFOLD [6]), model-contrastive local loss (MOON [7]), and normalized averaging (FedNova [8]) -- correcting the direction or magnitude of updates. A complementary and increasingly dominant line attacks it through privacy-preserving data augmentation. Central Mixup [3] and CutMix [23] regularize by interpolating training examples, but naive Mixup needs other clients' raw data, which the privacy contract forbids. Mean-Augmented Federated Learning (MAFL) / FedMix [17] resolves this: clients exchange only MEAN minibatches (xbar_j, ybar_j), and FedMix approximates the GLOBAL Mixup loss via a FIRST-ORDER TAYLOR expansion around those means, controlled by a single global mixup rate lambda. In plain ASCII the modified FedMix loss is:

    L_FedMix = (1 - lambda) * L(f((1 - lambda) x_i), y_i)
             + lambda       * L(f((1 - lambda) x_i), ybar_j)
             + lambda       * (dL/dx) . xbar_j

Because it injects augmentation signal while sending only means, FedMix is the reference recipe for Non-IID-robust augmentation, and personalized methods (pFedMe [11], Per-FedAvg [12], FedBN [13], clustered IFCA [18]) and federated HPO (FedEx [14]) surround it without displacing it.

What unifies the entire MAFL / FedMix augmentation line is that it is DIAGNOSTIC and uniform with respect to heterogeneity. FedMix broadcasts ONE global lambda to every client and treats the exchanged means purely as augmentation MATERIAL -- ingredients for the Taylor-approximated loss -- never as a heterogeneity SIGNAL. The same global mixing strength is applied to a near-IID client and to a severely label-skewed client alike. Fairness-oriented FL analyses [19,20] warn that policies tuned to the average client systematically underserve the off-distribution tail, yet the augmentation rate in FedMix and its descendants (including ensemble-distillation variants such as FedDF [21]) remains a fixed global constant. This sets up the gap addressed here.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

The gap is ISOMIX (the Isotropic-Mixing-rate Limit): FedMix's single global mixup rate lambda is REPRESENTATIONALLY INCAPABLE of expressing per-client, heterogeneity-conditional mixing. One scalar is broadcast and applied identically to every client regardless of how far that client's local label / class-mean distribution diverges from the global aggregate, so the deployed object simply cannot encode the per-client optimum that label-skewed and near-IID clients provably require to differ. Crucially, the harm is SILENT under pooled accuracy: a single lambda tuned to the average client UNDER-mixes the highest-divergence (most label-skewed) clients and OVER-mixes near-IID clients, and these two errors partially cancel in the pooled mean, so global accuracy looks healthy while the skewed-client tail and worst-client accuracy quietly degrade [4,19]. The deficit SCALES monotonically with label-skew divergence between client i's exchanged class-mean profile and the global mean profile, and VANISHES under IID, where the per-client optimum equals the global optimum (lambda*(d_i) = lambda_global for all i).

The method is CALIBER (Conditional Augmentation-rate caLIBration via Exchanged-mean Rate). CALIBER replaces the scalar lambda with a heterogeneity-conditional monotone map lambda(d_i), where d_i is the divergence of client i's label-proportion / class-mean profile from the global aggregate, computed ONLY from the means FedMix already exchanges (no new round, no new statistic, no extra leakage). The map is structured and monotone non-decreasing, estimated with HIERARCHICAL PARTIAL POOLING across heterogeneity bins [16,24], so data-poor clients shrink toward lambda_global instead of overfitting a private per-client value. CALIBER operates PURELY at the augmentation / loss layer: it changes only the value of lambda entering FedMix's existing L_FedMix, leaving the communication protocol, the mean-exchange step, and the privacy contract untouched.

CALIBER carries an EXACT-COLLAPSE identity. Clamping the conditional map to a constant -- lambda(d_i) = lambda_global for all i (the covariate-blind default, zero conditional gain) -- reduces CALIBER's per-client loss UPDATE-FOR-UPDATE to vanilla FedMix's L_FedMix. This is CI-gateable via

    E_collapse = max_i | L_CALIBER(i; lambda(d_i) = lambda_global) - L_FedMix(i) | <= 1e-6

so adoption is a do-no-harm shadow deployment with provable zero regression.

The novelty WEDGE has four clauses. C1: operates ONLY at the augmentation / loss layer, reusing FedMix's already-exchanged means, with no new round, no new exchanged statistic, and no change to the privacy contract (protocol- and comms-identical to FedMix). C2: conditions augmentation strength on an OBSERVABLE, privacy-preserving heterogeneity covariate d_i, not a fixed global constant. C3: models heterogeneity-conditional mixing with a STRUCTURED monotone map plus HIERARCHICAL PARTIAL POOLING, not a single global scalar and not an unpooled per-client free parameter. C4: provides PROVABLE exact collapse update-for-update to FedMix at the covariate-blind default, CI-gateable at E_collapse <= 1e-6. No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can CALIBER -- replacing FedMix's single global mixup rate lambda with a heterogeneity-conditional, partially-pooled map lambda(d_i) over an observable divergence covariate d_i computed only from the means FedMix already exchanges -- raise accuracy on the most label-skewed (highest-d_i) clients without altering the communication protocol or privacy contract, while provably collapsing update-for-update to vanilla FedMix at the covariate-blind default lambda(d) = lambda_global (E_collapse <= 1e-6, zero regression)?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 -- A broad blind spot in the dominant augmentation recipe. FedAvg [1] made local-data federated training practical, but its central pain is statistical heterogeneity: Non-IID client data makes local updates drift and degrades the averaged model [2,9], a failure named as a core open problem of the field [15]. The canonical privacy-preserving data-augmentation answer is MAFL / FedMix [17], which lifts central Mixup [3] into FL by exchanging only mean minibatches and Taylor-approximating the Mixup loss -- it is the reference recipe for Non-IID augmentation precisely because it adds signal without sending raw data. But FedMix broadcasts ONE global mixup rate lambda to every client. ISOMIX is the observation that a single scalar cannot express the per-client, heterogeneity-conditional optimal mixing strength that label-skewed and near-IID clients provably require to differ. Because FedMix is the dominant augmentation pattern, this is not a corner case in one paper -- it is a latent representational flaw inherited by the whole MAFL-style augmentation line.

P2 -- The harm is silent under pooled accuracy. The danger is that ISOMIX is metric-invisible on the standard dashboard. A single lambda tuned to the average client UNDER-mixes the highest-divergence clients and OVER-mixes near-IID clients; the two errors partially cancel in the pooled mean, so global accuracy can look healthy while the skewed-client tail and worst-client accuracy quietly degrade. Operators watching aggregate accuracy see nothing -- exactly the regime where heterogeneity surveys [4] warn that mean metrics mask per-client damage, and where worst-case and per-client evaluation [19,20] are needed to expose it. A flaw that does not show up on the metric everyone optimizes is a flaw that persists; that invisibility is what makes ISOMIX both important and easy to overlook.

P3 -- Fairness / client-level disparate impact. ISOMIX does not harm clients at random: by construction the deficit scales with label-skew divergence d_i, so the clients hurt most are the MOST heterogeneous -- rare-class holders, edge and minority populations, small-data hospitals whose local label profile is far from the global aggregate. That is a textbook federated-fairness concern: Agnostic FL [19] and q-Fair Federated Learning [20] argue that optimizing the average client systematically underserves the off-distribution tail, and the Non-IID literature [2,9,22] shows skew concentrates on exactly those clients. ISOMIX is a mechanism by which a "good average" augmentation policy delivers structurally worse augmentation to the populations with the least typical data -- a disparate-impact story, not just an accuracy story.

P4 -- Deployability: ships in shadow with provable zero regression. CALIBER is an adoptable drop-in, not merely an analysis. It operates PURELY at the augmentation / loss layer: it changes only the value of lambda fed into FedMix's existing approximated loss, leaving the mean-exchange step, the communication protocol, and the privacy contract untouched. The covariate d_i is computed for free from the means FedMix ALREADY exchanges -- so CALIBER turns those exchanged means from mere augmentation MATERIAL into a heterogeneity SIGNAL at no new comms or privacy cost. Crucially, at the covariate-blind default lambda(d) = lambda_global, CALIBER collapses update-for-update to vanilla FedMix, CI-gateable at E_collapse <= 1e-6. That exact-collapse identity converts adoption from a risky bet into a do-no-harm shadow deployment: a team can insert CALIBER, prove zero regression against FedMix, and only then turn up the conditional gain.

P5 -- Generality: a covariate-conditional-augmentation lens. The deeper interest is conceptual. FedMix sets its augmentation hyperparameter as a fixed global constant; CALIBER reframes it as a structured map CONDITIONED on an observable heterogeneity covariate. That lens is general. The same move applies to other federated hyperparameters tuned globally today -- FedProx's proximal weight mu [5], ensemble-distillation temperature [21], or local-epoch count -- and to other heterogeneity covariates beyond label skew (feature-skew and quantity-skew profiles, also derivable from exchanged statistics). It directly operationalizes the heterogeneous-FL survey's [4] call to handle statistical heterogeneity ADAPTIVELY rather than with one-size-fits-all constants, and it does so with a falsifiable, exactly-collapsing operator instead of an unverified heuristic.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 -- The client-conditional optimum is real but entangled. The per-client optimal mixing strength genuinely varies with heterogeneity, but that signal is confounded with local dataset size, local class count, and local task difficulty, so the "true" lambda*(d_i) is not directly observable. A naive per-client grid search overfits these nuisance factors and does not generalize across rounds or to held-out clients, while a single global lambda (FedMix) is provably unable to express the dependence at all -- one scalar cannot encode a function over d_i. The difficulty is to recover the heterogeneity-conditional part WITHOUT also fitting the noise.

O2 -- Estimation under severe data poverty. Most federated clients are tiny (tens to low hundreds of examples), so fitting an independent per-client lambda is high-variance and unstable, and the fitted value swings round-to-round with minibatch noise. Unpooled per-client tuning therefore both wastes the cross-client signal and degrades the data-poor clients it is meant to help. This is exactly why hierarchical PARTIAL POOLING [16,24] is required: data-poor clients must shrink toward lambda_global, and only clients with enough evidence move away from it.

O3 -- Observability and privacy of the covariate. The covariate d_i must be computable with NO new leakage and NO new exchanged statistic, or wedge C1 is broken and the privacy contract is violated. But the only objects FedMix already exchanges are first-moment means (xbar_j, ybar_j), and means discard higher-order structure (covariance, multimodality), so building a faithful heterogeneity divergence purely from means is non-trivial. Any covariate that needs raw data, a second round, or a new statistic is disqualified by construction -- the design space is constrained before tuning even begins.

O4 -- Multi-axis heterogeneity. Statistical heterogeneity is not a single axis: label skew, feature / covariate skew, and quantity skew act differently, so a scalar d_i can only be a proxy and may under-describe the true shift. The map lambda(d_i) must stay identifiable and robust when d_i is an imperfect, partially-observed proxy, rather than assuming d_i captures all heterogeneity. A method that silently assumes label skew is the whole story will misbehave on feature-skew-dominated clients.

O5 -- Simultaneous lift and exact collapse. CALIBER must add real corrections on high-d_i clients YET reduce update-for-update to FedMix at the clamp (lambda(d_i) = lambda_global). Making a genuine lift coexist with a CI-gateable E_collapse <= 1e-6 is a tight constraint: the parameterization must have an exact zero (g(0) = 0, theta = 0 implies identity) with no floating-point drift and correct monotonicity at the boundary. Many natural re-parameterizations (multiplicative gates, softmax-normalized maps, learned offsets) violate exact collapse because they cannot hit the baseline bit-for-bit.

O6 -- Metric blindness. The harm is invisible under pooled accuracy because under-mixing the skewed tail and over-mixing the near-IID clients partially cancel in the global mean, so evaluation itself is an obstacle. You must build disaggregated, divergence-stratified metrics (worst-K%, accuracy-vs-d_i slope, dispersion) or the lift cannot even be measured. A study that reports only global accuracy will falsely conclude "no effect" and miss the entire ISOMIX phenomenon.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The white space ISOMIX occupies is narrow and specific: a STRUCTURED, partially-pooled, MONOTONE augmentation-rate map conditioned on an OBSERVABLE heterogeneity covariate derived from FedMix's already-exchanged means, operating purely at the augmentation / loss layer, with a PROVABLE exact collapse to FedMix. Each prior family touches part of this and misses the rest; every near-miss explicitly fails at least two of the wedge clauses C1-C4.

Near-miss 1 -- Vanilla FedMix / MAFL (Yoon et al. [17]; central Mixup [3]; CutMix [23]). The direct ancestor: exchange mean minibatches, Taylor-approximate a global Mixup loss, broadcast ONE global mixup rate lambda. It satisfies C1 (augmentation / loss layer, reuses exchanged means) but the deployed object is a single global scalar applied identically to all clients. MISSES C2 (does not condition on any observable heterogeneity covariate; lambda is covariate-blind), C3 (a global scalar, not a structured partially-pooled monotone map), and offers no C4 gain because it IS the collapse target, not a method that collapses to it.

Near-miss 2 -- Optimizer / aggregation-layer Non-IID correction (FedProx [5], SCAFFOLD [6], FedNova [8], MOON [7]). These fight client drift -- proximal terms, control variates, normalized averaging, model-contrastive local regularization -- and do condition on heterogeneity in a loose sense. But they act at the optimizer / aggregation layer, not the augmentation layer, and they correct update DIRECTION / MAGNITUDE, never augmentation STRENGTH. MISSES C1 (wrong layer; they change the optimizer / aggregator, not FedMix's augmentation loss, and do not reuse the exchanged means as an augmentation signal), C2 (do not condition augmentation strength on a divergence covariate), and C4 (no exact update-for-update collapse to FedMix).

Near-miss 3 -- Personalized / per-client FL (pFedMe [11], Per-FedAvg [12], FedBN [13], clustered FL / IFCA [18]). These adapt the MODEL per client -- Moreau-envelope personalization, meta-initialization, per-client BatchNorm statistics, or cluster-specific models -- and so they vary behavior across clients. But they personalize model parameters, not a heterogeneity-conditional AUGMENTATION-RATE map; their per-client adaptation is typically unpooled or cluster-discrete, not a structured monotone map shrunk by partial pooling toward a global default; and none defines an observable divergence covariate from exchanged means or a collapse to FedMix. MISSES C2, C3, and C4.

Near-miss 4 -- Federated hyperparameter optimization (FedEx weight-sharing HPO [14]). This DOES tune FL hyperparameters -- including, in principle, augmentation or local hyperparameters -- and can produce client- or round-varying values. But it treats the hyperparameter as the target of a SEARCH / weight-sharing exponentiated-gradient procedure, not as a closed-form map from an observable heterogeneity covariate; the resulting schedule is not a structured monotone function of d_i, and it carries no exact-collapse guarantee to FedMix. MISSES C2 (tunes by search, not by conditioning on an observable d_i covariate), C3 (search output is not a structured partially-pooled monotone map), and C4 (no provable update-for-update collapse).

Near-miss 5 -- Local / generative data augmentation and federated distillation (Mixup [3] and CutMix [23] applied locally; GAN-based federated augmentation; ensemble distillation FedDF [21]). These add augmentation or knowledge-transfer signal, but at GLOBAL / UNIFORM strength: a fixed mixing / temperature constant, or synthetic data generated without conditioning on each client's divergence from the global profile, with no calibration to label skew. MISSES C2 (uniform strength, no heterogeneity-conditional calibration), C3 (no structured monotone partially-pooled map), and C4 (no exact collapse to FedMix).

No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4. Two assumptions quietly foreclosed the fix. First, the means FedMix exchanges were treated ONLY as augmentation MATERIAL -- ingredients for the Taylor-approximated loss -- and never as a heterogeneity SIGNAL; the divergence between a client's own profile and the global profile sits in data FedMix already moves, but nobody read those means as an observable covariate d_i on which to CONDITION lambda, so the augmentation rate stayed a single broadcast scalar. Second, the harm was hidden: because ISOMIX's under- and over-mixing errors cancel in pooled accuracy [4,19], the dominant metric showed no problem, so there was no pressure to replace the scalar. Personalization research [11,12,13,18] adapted MODELS, optimizer research [5,6,7,8] adapted UPDATES, and HPO research [14] SEARCHED hyperparameters -- but the specific object (a structured, partially-pooled [16,24], monotone augmentation-rate map over a free, exchanged-mean divergence covariate, with provable FedMix collapse) fell in the gap between all of them. CALIBER differs by occupying exactly that intersection: same layer and same comms as FedMix, conditioned on a free observable d_i, structured-monotone and partially pooled, and provably collapsing to FedMix.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Method formalization. For each client i, form the local label-proportion vector phat_i (class frequencies derived from its own exchanged ybar_i) and, optionally, the local class-mean profile from xbar_i. Form the global aggregate pbar as the client-weighted mean of the exchanged ybar_j over participating clients. Define the divergence covariate

    d_i = || phat_i - pbar ||          (L2 default, total-variation alternative)

with d_i in [0, d_max] and d_i = 0 under IID (phat_i = pbar). d_i is normalized to a common range (divide by d_max, or use total variation which is already in [0,1]) so heterogeneity is comparable across datasets. d_i is computed ONLY from means FedMix already exchanges -- no new round, statistic, or leakage (C1, C2). Replace the scalar lambda by a low-parameter MONOTONE map

    lambda(d_i) = clamp_[0,1]( lambda_global + g_theta(d_i) ),   g_theta monotone non-decreasing, g_theta(0) = 0.

A concrete instance is g_theta(d) = theta * d (or a 2-3 knot monotone spline / isotonic fit), so lambda(d_i) increases with heterogeneity from the shared anchor lambda_global. Parameters theta are estimated by HIERARCHICAL PARTIAL POOLING [16,24]: per-heterogeneity-bin slopes / intercepts are random effects drawn around the global anchor, so data-poor clients shrink toward lambda_global and only well-evidenced bins depart from it (C3; addresses O1, O2). Exact collapse: clamping g_theta = 0 or theta = 0 gives lambda(d_i) = lambda_global for all i, and since the only change is the scalar entering the existing L_FedMix, CALIBER reduces update-for-update to vanilla FedMix, gated by E_collapse = max_i | L_CALIBER(i; lambda(d_i) = lambda_global) - L_FedMix(i) | <= 1e-6 (C4). Protocol, comms, and privacy contract are unchanged.

Datasets and heterogeneity simulation. CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 with Dirichlet(alpha) label partitions [10], sweeping alpha in {0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, IID} to CONTROL d_i (small alpha implies large d_i; IID implies d_i = 0); 100 clients, 10% participation per round; models are a 3-block CNN and ResNet-18. FEMNIST (LEAF [25]): natural Non-IID by writer, ~3400 clients, subsample ~200-500 per round at low participation, small CNN. Shakespeare (LEAF [25]): natural Non-IID by speaking role, next-character prediction, 2-layer LSTM. Optional Federated EMNIST and CINIC-10 for an additional Non-IID source and a larger label space. Participation 5-10%, local epochs E = 1-5, report over 3 seeds.

Baselines. B1 Local-only (no FL, lower reference); B2 FedAvg (standard FL baseline); B3 FedProx (heterogeneity-aware baseline); B4 Vanilla FedMix (THE exact-collapse target; CALIBER must reduce to this); B5 Per-client grid-tuned lambda ORACLE (compute-intensive and impractical -- it is NOT a deployable method and exists only to upper-bound the achievable client-conditional gap); B6 Global-best single lambda / well-tuned FedMix (strongest single-scalar competitor, rules out "CALIBER just tuned lambda better"); B7 CALIBER (ours). B4 is the collapse object, B5 establishes headroom, B6 is the fair single-scalar bar to beat on the tail.

Metrics. Global / pooled accuracy must be >= FedMix (no regression). WORST-K% accuracy (worst-20% high-d_i clients) is the PRIMARY metric. Also accuracy-vs-d_i slope / divergence-stratified accuracy per d_i bin; client-accuracy dispersion / fairness gap (max-min, variance across clients); communication + privacy cost (must be identical to FedMix, 0% overhead); and E_collapse at the clamp (<= 1e-6).

Ablations. A1 constant lambda vs conditional lambda(d_i) (isolates conditioning); A2 pooled vs unpooled estimation (isolates partial pooling; O2); A3 monotone vs free / unconstrained map (isolates the monotone prior; O5); A4 d_i choice -- L2 vs TV vs cosine, label-only vs label+feature-mean; A5 sensitivity to Dirichlet alpha (how lift scales with heterogeneity); A6 robustness when d_i is a NOISY proxy (inject feature / quantity skew not captured by phat_i; tests O4 identifiability).

Exact-collapse CI gate and falsifiability. A build / CI test clamps theta = 0 (lambda(d_i) = lambda_global for all i), runs N forward / backward updates on fixed batches against vanilla FedMix, and ASSERTS E_collapse = max_i | L_CALIBER(i) - L_FedMix(i) | <= 1e-6; the build fails otherwise, so exact collapse is enforced, not assumed. Three falsifiability triples (manipulation -> measurable outcome -> refuter): T1 IID NULL -- on IID partitions d_i = 0 for all i so lambda(d_i) = lambda_global; predict CALIBER == FedMix within seed noise, and a statistically significant IID accuracy gap (either sign) REFUTES the "lift comes from heterogeneity-conditioning" mechanism. T2 MONOTONE-DOSE -- sweep alpha from IID down to 0.05 (increasing d_i); predict CALIBER's worst-20% lift over FedMix is ~0 at IID and grows monotonically as d_i grows, and a flat or non-monotone lift-vs-d_i curve REFUTES that the gain is driven by d_i. T3 COVARIATE-SCRAMBLE -- randomly permute d_i across clients (break the true heterogeneity-to-client mapping) while keeping the map fixed; predict the worst-20% lift collapses to ~0, and if scrambled-d_i retains the lift the gain is NOT from the covariate and CALIBER's mechanism is REFUTED.

Quantitative hypotheses (ranges). H1: worst-20% high-d_i client accuracy +3 to +8 points over FedMix at alpha <= 0.1 (largest at alpha = 0.05). H2: pooled / global accuracy within +/- 0.3 points of FedMix (no regression) and ~0 change at IID. H3: fairness gap (max-min across clients) reduced by 15% to 40% vs FedMix under high heterogeneity. H4: accuracy-vs-d_i slope -- FedMix strongly negative, CALIBER flattened toward 0, confirming tail recovery. H5: E_collapse <= 1e-6 by construction at the clamp (CI-enforced). H6: communication + privacy overhead 0% (identical to FedMix).

Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations (Rai folds). F1 -- Privacy surface: CALIBER inherits the privacy surface of MAFL / FedMix's mean-minibatch exchange; even adding no new statistic, exchanged per-client means can enable membership or attribute inference, especially for small or single-class clients where the label-proportion profile phat_i is quasi-identifying. Responsible deployment should pair CALIBER with secure aggregation of the exchanged means, calibrated differential-privacy noise on those means, and a minimum-cohort threshold (e.g., n_i >= 10) before computing d_i. F2 -- Demographic-proxy governance: if clients correspond to identifiable populations (hospitals, regions, user cohorts), d_i may correlate with protected attributes; production use should include a governance review of whether per-client lambda differentiation amounts to protected-class-correlated treatment, and otherwise substitute a cohort-aggregated covariate for per-client d_i. F3 -- Auditability: because lambda(d_i) differs per client, log lambda(d_i) and d_i per client per round and document an explicit collapse / disable switch (clamp theta = 0, CI-gated by E_collapse) as the operator's first recourse if differential treatment is contested. F4 -- Fairness floor: the exact-collapse property (E_collapse <= 1e-6 at theta = 0) is CALIBER's do-no-harm fairness floor -- no client is worse off than under vanilla FedMix at the covariate-blind default, CI-enforceable and shadow-deployable before any conditional gain is activated.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] H. B. McMahan, E. Moore, D. Ramage, S. Hampson, B. A. y Arcas. "Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data." AISTATS 2017.

[2] Y. Zhao, M. Li, L. Lai, N. Suda, D. Civin, V. Chandra. "Federated Learning with Non-IID Data." arXiv:1806.00582, 2018.

[3] H. Zhang, M. Cisse, Y. N. Dauphin, D. Lopez-Paz. "mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization." ICLR 2018.

[4] M. Ye, X. Fang, B. Du, P. C. Yuen, D. Tao. "Heterogeneous Federated Learning: State-of-the-Art and Research Challenges." ACM Computing Surveys, 2023. arXiv:2307.10616.

[5] T. Li, A. K. Sahu, M. Zaheer, M. Sanjabi, A. Talwalkar, V. Smith. "Federated Optimization in Heterogeneous Networks (FedProx)." MLSys 2020.

[6] S. P. Karimireddy, S. Kale, M. Mohri, S. J. Reddi, S. U. Stich, A. T. Suresh. "SCAFFOLD: Stochastic Controlled Averaging for Federated Learning." ICML 2020.

[7] Q. Li, B. He, D. Song. "Model-Contrastive Federated Learning (MOON)." CVPR 2021.

[8] J. Wang, Q. Liu, H. Liang, G. Joshi, H. V. Poor. "Tackling the Objective Inconsistency Problem in Heterogeneous Federated Optimization (FedNova)." NeurIPS 2020.

[9] T. Li, A. K. Sahu, A. Talwalkar, V. Smith. "Federated Learning: Challenges, Methods, and Future Directions." IEEE Signal Processing Magazine, 2020.

[10] T.-M. H. Hsu, H. Qi, M. Brown. "Measuring the Effects of Non-Identical Data Distribution for Federated Visual Classification." arXiv:1909.06335, 2019.

[11] C. T. Dinh, N. H. Tran, T. D. Nguyen. "Personalized Federated Learning with Moreau Envelopes (pFedMe)." NeurIPS 2020.

[12] A. Fallah, A. Mokhtari, A. Ozdaglar. "Personalized Federated Learning: A Meta-Learning Approach (Per-FedAvg)." NeurIPS 2020.

[13] X. Li, M. Jiang, X. Zhang, M. Kamp, Q. Dou. "FedBN: Federated Learning on Non-IID Features via Local Batch Normalization." ICLR 2021.

[14] M. Khodak, R. Tu, T. Li, L. Li, M.-F. Balcan, V. Smith, A. Talwalkar. "Federated Hyperparameter Tuning: Challenges, Baselines, and Connections to Weight-Sharing (FedEx)." NeurIPS 2021.

[15] P. Kairouz, H. B. McMahan, et al. "Advances and Open Problems in Federated Learning." Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning, 2021. arXiv:1912.04977.

[16] A. Gelman, J. Hill. "Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models." Cambridge University Press, 2007.

[17] T. Yoon, S. Shin, S. J. Hwang, E. Yang. "FedMix: Approximation of Mixup under Mean Augmented Federated Learning." ICLR 2021.

[18] A. Ghosh, J. Chung, D. Yin, K. Ramchandran. "An Efficient Framework for Clustered Federated Learning (IFCA)." NeurIPS 2020.

[19] M. Mohri, G. Sivek, A. T. Suresh. "Agnostic Federated Learning." ICML 2019.

[20] T. Li, M. Sanjabi, A. Beirami, V. Smith. "Fair Resource Allocation in Federated Learning (q-FFL)." ICLR 2020.

[21] T. Lin, L. Kong, S. U. Stich, M. Jaggi. "Ensemble Distillation for Robust Model Fusion in Federated Learning (FedDF)." NeurIPS 2020.

[22] K. Hsieh, A. Phanishayee, O. Mutlu, P. B. Gibbons. "The Non-IID Data Quagmire of Decentralized Machine Learning." ICML 2020.

[23] S. Yun, D. Han, S. J. Oh, S. Chun, J. Choe, Y. Yoo. "CutMix: Regularization Strategy to Train Strong Classifiers with Localizable Features." ICCV 2019.

[24] B. Efron, C. Morris. "Stein's Estimation Rule and Its Competitors -- An Empirical Bayes Approach." Journal of the American Statistical Association, 1973.

[25] S. Caldas, S. M. K. Duddu, P. Wu, T. Li, J. Konecny, H. B. McMahan, V. Smith, A. Talwalkar. "LEAF: A Benchmark for Federated Settings." arXiv:1812.01097, 2018.
